lines.text: Section seven. Standard DNA assembly and the BioBricks story.
If parts-devices-systems is the conceptual framework, standard DNA assembly is the physical machinery that implements it.
Engineering disciplines outside biology run on standards — USB plugs, screw threads, voltage levels, transistor pinouts.
These standards exist because they let parts from different sources be combined without bespoke adaptation.
Synthetic biology's analogous problem is: how do you connect two pieces of DNA reliably, repeatably, and in a way that the entire community agrees on?
The first attempts at this used unique restriction enzyme recognition sites — short DNA sequences cut by specific restriction enzymes.
If you put a unique site at each boundary of each part, you can cut and paste parts together using standard cloning techniques.
This works, but it is fragile.
Each new part needs a different combination of flanking sites.
Adding the sites requires PCR or other DNA modification, which is laborious.
And the available unique sites are a finite pool — your assembly will eventually run out.
In 2003, Tom Knight at MIT proposed a fundamentally different convention, which became known as the BioBricks assembly standard.

The key insight is to use a single, standard set of restriction enzymes at every part boundary — specifically, three particular enzymes — and to design the cutting chemistry so that joining two BioBricks parts produces a new BioBricks part that can be joined to the next one in turn.

Mechanically, the cleverness involves what biochemists call isocaudomers — different restriction enzymes whose digested overhangs can ligate together, even though the enzymes themselves recognise different sites.

Using these isocaudomers, you can cut two BioBricks parts with different enzymes, ligate them together, and end up with a product where the original recognition sites are gone but a new set of sites is present at the boundaries.
The product is itself a BioBricks part, ready for further assembly.
This is exactly the recursive, hierarchical, scar-controlled assembly that the parts-devices-systems framework requires.
The BioBricks standard is detailed in Appendix 1 of the textbook for those who want the exact mechanics, but the principle is what matters for now.
BioBricks made it possible for any researcher, anywhere in the world, with a single set of common restriction enzymes, to reliably combine any two BioBricks parts.
This is the foundation that the iGEM competition is built on — undergraduates in summer programs assembling complex devices from BioBricks parts in just three months.
And it underlies the iGEM parts registry, which we will return to shortly.
BioBricks has limitations the authors are honest about.
Each assembly step leaves behind a small "scar" — a few extra base pairs between adjacent parts — that can affect the function of neighbouring parts.
Parts that contain the restriction-enzyme recognition sites used by the BioBricks standard internally cannot be used without modification.
And the methodology is slow compared with newer techniques like Gibson assembly or Golden Gate.
For these reasons, professional synthetic biology projects today often use bespoke or scarless assembly methods rather than strict BioBricks.

But the conceptual contribution of BioBricks remains: it was the first widely-adopted standard for biological-parts assembly, and it normalised the idea that the field could agree on a common interface.
Whatever the assembly method, the cultural shift it enabled is permanent.
